paragraphs.body: The Night Out
Short Story 2, 1st Draft
Classic Time

It is just as loud as Olivia feared, if not louder. The bass of club music, early 2000s electronic pop and scratched with the DJ’s offensive touch, stinging the inside of her eardrums. The noise rattles through her head, impounding on her teeth. As the music grows louder and faster, so do the bodies that move with increased excitement, pushing their tattooed elbows and forearms into Olivia’s nose: her vision an array of dancing inked moths and angel numbers. Soon, the bodies’ faces follow, and inches from her face are blurry features, indistinguishable in the screaming LED lights. A myriad of titanium facial jewelry flashing purple or blue. Pink Manic Panic frayed and thinning hair. And the smell of sweat. God, the stench of sweat, hardly masked by aluminum-free deodorant.

annotations.comment: This made me laugh, in a good way.

paragraphs.body: Olivia scrunches her face tightly, feeling the skin of her cheeks in her eyes. Running her fingertips along the droplets on her hairline, somebody hip checks her. She stumbles, but catches herself, the culprit long gone. She grabs on to her friend June’s shoulder, half to steady herself, and half to get her attention. Olivia pulls June’s ear toward her mouth, and makes a futile attempt at communication.
“I’m going to go to the bathroom,” Olivia says, louder than usual, careful to enunciate.
“What?” June yells back, her face contorting itself in tipsy confusion. June barely even looks in Olivia’s direction, only yelps into the crowd.
“Bathroom,”
“What?”

annotations.comment: You convey this world very well. I hope that I find out why Olivia is there if she hates it so.

paragraphs.body: They could go on like this for ages, the synthetic beats and drops overpowering their words. Olivia could wait for the DJ to switch the track to announce her departure, savor the moment of stillness amongst so much noise. Instead, Olivia sighs, a faint sound only visible to her, and pushes June’s shoulder back into the sweating elbows and hands. Olivia weaves herself across the dance floor, letting the quick screech of the music hum into background noise.

annotations.comment: Sound can’t be visible
I would think that going to the bathroom could be conveyed by pointing, so it’s interesting that she doesn’t even try that.

paragraphs.body: It is not the bathroom she wants, but the freeze of December’s chill against her exposed skin. The gay bar always has dancing on the weekends, but oddly tonight, in the middle of Georgia’s winter, was oddly popular. Perhaps, it is the deep feeling of loneliness during the holidays that moves people into celebration. Or, Olivia thinks, pushing past girls in candy cane striped flare pants and shirtless boys layered in tinsel, it’s just actual celebration. And she is projecting. Just a little bit.

annotations.comment: I like the way this undercuts the slight pretension of our main character.

paragraphs.body: Olivia emerges from the sea of pulsing clubbers at the stairs. She walks down, listening to the music turn into a soft whimper. Finding the door, she pushes against the glass, excited to feel the fifty degree wind cooling her skin. The bouncer stops her.
“No re-entry, m’am. Just so you know.”

The bouncer, a short gay man with blue liquid eyeliner and patchy highlighter draped across his cheekbone and cupid’s bow, crosses his arms as if signifying some level of authority; yet, Olivia feels more so just annoyed, and sorry for insufficient blending skills in whichever one of his friends attempted his makeup.
Maybe he did it himself and he feels proud. Olivia feels bad at this thought, and she judges herself silently in her head for being so cruel.

annotations.comment: This character is very in her own head. It works nicely. I also appreciated the clarity of her want — to be outside, away, but briefly.

paragraphs.body: “C’mon, man,” Olivia says, groaning, “I just want some air.”

This is partially true. Some air and an Uber back to her apartment. Although, she doesn’t really know what she was thinking heading for the door. She shouldn’t leave June alone in the club. By the time it takes Olivia to argue with the bouncer, June could have pounded back 3 more Fireball eggnog shots – which Olivia says is gross and bastardizing the spirit of Christmas, but either way would make June less likely to get home on her own.

annotations.text: She shouldn’t leave June alone in the club
annotations.comment: I appreciate the solidarity of this as well. We all have that friend.

paragraphs.body: The bouncer sighs, rolling his eyes at her, “Sorry, girl. You can go, but you’ll have to pay the cover again to get back in.”

“Pay the cover again? It’s $20!”
	“Sorry. I don’t make the rules.”

Olivia huffs. Upstairs, the music reverberates off the walls, flooding to a soft muffle from where she stands. She stares at the bouncer for a few seconds, debating whether she could walk out and sneak around the back, or disarm him, drug him using the lone prescription Xanax in her purse, and hide him underneath the bar. Or the janitors closet. This also makes her feel quite bad. The poor kid is just doing his job.

annotations.text: The poor kid is just doing his job.
annotations.comment: I really read him as older than her, just FYI.

paragraphs.body: Instead, she hikes back up the stairs to find June, the increasing crescendo of electropop cutting through the background of her hero’s journey.

The gay club on a Friday night in December is not Olivia’s first pick for the evening. Truthfully, she would rather prefer to be in her apartment, long pajamas on, making wedding soup in her heart shaped dutch oven and turning her head away from the stove every few minutes to analyze last week’s episode of The Bachelorette. She likes The Bachelorette more than The Bachelor because it’s funny to see men act stupid over one girl, whereas watching multiple girls fight over one man feels inherently non-feminist and makes her think too hard about her own role in perpetuating misogyny by ingesting such content.

annotations.comment: I’d give this club a name. “The gay club” feels a little harsh after the second time of saying it.

paragraphs.body: But June wanted to go. Her argument was indestructible:
“I’m a Sagittarius,” June said, “I have to dance!”
And, June is her best friend from undergrad. It was decided.

annotations.comment: Love this moment and the dialogue. I did wonder if she would maybe tell the bouncer some of this?

paragraphs.body: Olivia is a Capricorn and trying not to think about hearing back about interviews for her PhD applications. Her CV was perfect: four years of research experience in undergrad studying rumination as a transdiagnostic predictor for generalized anxiety and obsessive-compulsive disorder in young adults to aid in early intervention strategies, two gap years, encompassing a crisis hotline volunteer experience and a full-time job as a caseworker, an undergraduate thesis in the neural mechanisms of anxiety and brain activity in active worry. Her letters of recommendation are shining. Her personal statement is flawless. She is a beacon of hope in the field. A young southern girl who is going to make her parents really, really proud.
All she has to do is wait.

annotations.comment: Like this little sketch of her. I thought the undergrad thesis mention should come before the gap years. That threw me off. Or it is a master’s thesis?
annotations.context_left: ll she has to do is

paragraphs.body: And she was waiting. Olivia waited in front of The Bachelorette, refreshing her Gmail app in-between rose ceremonies. She waited as the wedding soup rose to a boil. She waited in the line at the Farmer’s Market. She waited in the window seat on the Marta on the way to work.
She didn’t want to wait at the club.

annotations.text: She didn’t want to wait at the club
annotations.comment: I get it, but surely she knows she will not hear on a Saturday night, right? I mean, that can be its own torture, now you have to wait 48 hours until you could possible hear anything. But that’s not quite clear yet. You might consider whether she leaves to check her phone as well as to cool down.

paragraphs.body: Olivia drags herself upstairs, the heaviness of her ears filling again with song, a pressure so tense that she feels she is underwater. Olivia scans the dance floor for June, but can’t locate her among those same warm bodies who swarmed around her like killer bees moments ago.
Instead, Olivia finds June by the bar. The shot she slams back doesn’t look like the milky white eggnog. But, the basis of Olivia’s theory is right. She will be plastered soon.
Olivia rushes over to her, “June.”
“Oh-my-god-hey-girl-hey! Where were you?”

Olivia shakes her head, signifying to her friend that she doesn’t want to waste time on the question. She sits herself on the bar seat beside her, and waves the bartender off as he looks at her with a raised eyebrow.

Olivia runs her hand along the nape of her neck, her skin still hot and prickled with sweat, “When do you want to leave?”
	“Leave?” June says, “We just got here!”
“Like, two hours ago.”
“Exactly!” June slides the bartender a five, which he swiftly slips into the pocket of his skinny jeans. She pushes the shot glass away from her, and then clasps her hand against Olivia’s wrist.

annotations.text: “Exactly!” June slides the bartender a five, which he swiftly slips into the pocket of his skinny jeans
annotations.comment: In my experience, bartenders at gay bars either LOVE or HATE straight girls partying.

paragraphs.body: “Look,” June says. She leans in close, and Olivia can smell the alcohol on the inside of her mouth. Too sterile to be Fireball. It didn’t have that cinnamon smell. Maybe vodka.

“I met someone,” June whispers, nodding her head toward the dance floor. She looks away slightly so as to not give away their secret undercover pow-wow, “Pink shirt. Over there. Don’t make it obvious. Olivia. You’re making it obvious. Chill!”

The girl is tall and slender, with muscles peeking through her cropped pink-shirt, the same bright cherry color as her hair. She’s dancing around a group of a few others, screaming the lyrics to a 2012 radio hit with unrivaled passion. The girl peers back over at June, smiling. Olivia thinks she looks nice. Sweet, even. You would probably have to be in order to pull off hair that color.

annotations.comment: Oh, is it a lesbian bar?
annotations.context_left: rl is tall and slender, with muscles peeking through her cropped pink-shirt, the same bright cherry
annotations.context_right: as her hair. She’s dancing around a group of a few others, screaming the lyrics to a 2012 radio hit

paragraphs.body: “Well, you should talk to her. Maybe you two will hit it off,” Olivia says, patting her friend on the back twice, “And I can go home.”

June rolls her eyes, “Dude,” she says, as if it’s a full statement. She puts her hands on her hips. When Olivia doesn’t react, June grabs onto her shoulders, and brings their faces close together again.
“You are so fucking obsessed with grad school.”
“What? What does that have to do with you getting laid?”
“Everything. We should be having fun! When you get into grad school –”
“If,”

“Like I said. When you get into grad school, you’re going to be so busy. This is our last chance to really have fun together,” Olivia pushes her friend away from her dramatically to emphasize her point, “And then who knows where we will be.”

Olivia frowns. Again, she feels bad. Her therapist would remind her that her fixation on getting into graduate school is inhibiting her daily functioning, and Olivia would say that she knows, but she doesn’t particularly care. She still gets on the bus, talks to clients at work, and prepares herself meals. She still wakes up early, goes for her daily run, and calls her parents on the weekends. If the inhibition of her functioning makes her motivated to start her future in academia, maybe it isn’t all that bad. Even if it comes with the side effect of itching to go home and stare at her email every time she is in public. Perhaps, this is even a good thing. Isn’t partying generally considered a maladaptive behavior? Really, it’s rude of her friends to try and draw her away from her future.

annotations.comment: The fact that she’s thinking about it means that she does care, so this was confusing. I wasn’t sure if Olivia would be lying to herself or to her therapist or…

paragraphs.body: Olivia shakes her head, “I’m sorry, June. I know you’re right,”
“I always am.”

Olivia looks across the room, toward the girl with the pink hair. She’s swaying her hips, raising her drink with an extended arm to the ceiling. Her friends interlock their arms around her neck, and they raise their drinks to lipstick smudged mouths together.

Olivia frowns at this image, even though she recognizes the girl is quite beautiful, and her laughter, although silenced from the loud music and distance apart, is probably also beautiful. It makes her sad in a way that she can’t explain.
“I do really have to go, though,” June says. She takes out her phone and pretends to look at the time, an attempt to avoid her friend’s gaze, “Call me if you need a ride home.”
Olivia leaves her friend at the bar, hurries downstairs, and walks past the bouncer with the bad makeup. When she floods into the street, the music is only a soft groan in the distance.

annotations.comment: Wanted more from this moment. Again, I would love to factor in the fact that she is absolutely not going to get an acceptance email at 2 AM. Yet she continues the obsession — which is fine! Works for the story — but she’s too smart not to understand this.

paragraphs.body: Atlanta at night is almost as loud as the inside of the club, with just as many multi-colored flashing lights. Olivia is relieved to feel the sting of a southern winter on her skin, but the guilt of leaving her friend behind sucks away any of the original pleasure that could have been derived from it. She starts walking toward the bus stop, and then decides to pull her phone out of her pocket and refresh her Gmail again, like an obsessive baby that needs her compulsion pacifier. Nothing. She checks her texts from June:
junebug <3: :( awww come back!!
junebug <3: girl plssssss
junebug <3: ill buy u a drink???

Olivia puts her phone back in her pocket. To June’s defense, the night is still early. It is only a little past midnight, but it means that Olivia can catch the last bus back to her apartment. As she trudges across the sidewalk, she hugs herself in her arms, almost laughing at how quickly she began to miss the body heat circling through the club. She feels self conscious without a jacket, to keep her warm but also make her feel secure. She remembers the Pink Girl’s friends’ throwing their arms around her neck.

annotations.comment: I think having this realization — I can still get the bus home! — in the club would help explain her sudden departure. We know she wants to go but the story needs a trigger that makes her go.
How did they get there? Was she cold then?

paragraphs.body: There are only a few late night stragglers at the stop waiting to catch the bus. An old woman using a cane, dressed in a long, furry trench coat. A middle aged man in a puffer jacket. A college-aged girl in scrubs and headphones. Some scroll through their phones, eyes darting up every few moments to assess a potential threat of danger, and then retreating back into the virtual abyss. Some just stare straight ahead.

Olivia sits down at the stop, crossing her legs and running her hands along the goosebumps on her arms. She closes her eyes briefly, and tries to rehearse her breathing before remembering the Xanax in her purse. Plopping the pill in her mouth, she leans back. The Atlanta night sky is endless, and she stares into it, letting the medicated numbness hurdle over her.

The bus screeches to a halt at the stop only moments later, and Olivia lifts herself on. She takes the aisle seat next to the old woman with the cane, and watches as she attempts to try and balance it in between her legs. Olivia wonders, briefly, why she wouldn’t use the disability seating in the front, but this thought merely passes over her. A big thanks in part to her psychiatrist and his magical ability to prescribe.

Such magical powers of prescription work too well when Olivia’s head begins to fall to its side, her reflexes stirring her awake each time, until the movement of her brief time dancing in the club begins to divide with the medication. She drifts into a light sleep on the woman’s shoulder. When Olivia is awoken by the bus’s brakes, embarrassment zips through her.
“Oh my god,” Olivia says, “I’m so sorry.”

The woman laughs, showing off her teeth when she does. They are white and straight, either dentures or veneers, but they suit the structure of her face perfectly. The woman’s eyes are sunken, lines forming beside them, and lines around her mouth and across her forehead. As Olivia nears twenty-five, she becomes increasingly aware of the wrinkles on everyone’s faces, and sometimes spends time comparing them to her own, the small indents beginning to form. She hates this about herself. It makes her feel so judgmental.

annotations.comment: I confess that I’m a little lost on where the story can go now. She got what she wanted. She left.

paragraphs.body: “Oh, that’s alright, honey,” the woman says, still laughing. Olivia notices that her hair, short and shaven down, is dyed a deep purple, matching the acrylics on her fingernails, “Long night?”
Olivia laughs, comforted, “Something like that.”
The woman’s laughter unsteadies the cane from in between her legs, and she adjusts it, “Yeah, I bet. I remember.”

Olivia assumes the woman means I remember what that was like for me, although the way it comes to her in a medicated haze makes her think that she means it like a time traveler coming from the future. Xanax and one Cosmopolitan, a bad combination.
The woman must see the confusion on Olivia’s face, because she clarifies, “I was a bit of a party girl. Once. Long, long time ago.”

Olivia laughs faintly, finding it funny that the woman can read the expression on her face enough to clarify, “Yeah. That’s like my friend June,” she brushes through her hair, picking out tangles from where she fell asleep on the woman’s shoulder, “Not so much me.”
“Uh-huh,” the woman says, diverting her eyes to Olivia’s skimpy black dress and then up toward her face which, she presumes, features her eyeliner smeared in across her cheeks.
Olivia laughs again, tucking the clumped strands of hair behind her ear, “Oh, yeah. I don’t know. My friend wanted to dance. I’d rather be at home, though.”

annotations.comment: I like this dialogue but it does feel like it’s reasserting what we already know.

paragraphs.body: More bodies flood onto the bus with every stop. Mainly elderly people, choosing to sit in the front or pushing toward the back. The earlier buses usually have students from Georgia State, traveling in groups, talking loudly and unaware of how they position themselves to take up too much space. Tonight, the bus is quiet, the occupied seating sparse.
The woman ignores the last part of Olivia’s reply, “Your friend?”

annotations.comment: I didn’t quite follow why the woman said that.

paragraphs.body: Olivia blushes. She fiddles with the ring on her hand, suddenly nervous that the woman, through her potential psychic powers, can tell that Olivia is a bad, self-obsessed friend only interested in engaging in ritualistic checking behaviors regarding her prospective graduate education and shitty reality TV that doesn’t require critical thinking.
“Yeah,” Olivia says, “Like I said. A real party girl.”

The woman nods. Olivia wants her to say that she understands. That she, too, engaged in Atlanta’s nightlife and felt allured by the bright lights and loud music, but now she is serious. A retired career woman. An ex-academic who has contributed widely to her field. She gave all of that up long ago. She had to. It was the only way. She has purple, shaven hair as a testament to those old times, earlier days, when she could be young and multicolored. But she is serious, just like Olivia. Just like Olivia is and will soon be.

annotations.comment: A nice moment internally and then the deflation of reality works.

paragraphs.body: Instead, the woman says, “This is my stop.”

Olivia gets up so that the woman can get past her, and she watches the woman exit the back door, flooding into the darkness of the night, her cane tapping against the sidewalk for only a moment until the doors close. The air that floods onto the bus is cold, and it stings Olivia’s exposed knees. The neighborhood is darker than the sky, burnt out street lights every few feet.
In her lap, Olivia’s phone lights up. She has two notifications. A missed call from June, and a new, unread email in her inbox.

Olivia takes a deep breath. She looks out the window, not toward Atlanta’s sky, but the silhouette of the woman’s body, darkened by a lack of light, limping slowly toward a small house. Her trench coat dangles to the ground, and, despite the darkness, Olivia can see the reflection of moonlight on her body.
She unlocks her phone and dials her friend.

annotations.comment: I really like the ending, and the choice between these two forces pulling on Olivia. It strikes me that neither of these are particularly healthy or good — the obsessive pursuit of a doctorate, the friend flirting with danger. It’s potent. And I think this is a strong story — Olivia is a hard character to write, someone so smart and yet stuck in her own head but you pulled it off. I did feel that you’re not yet making great use of the other people in the story (with the exception of the woman at the end). The bouncer, the pink girl, they really could interact with Olivia in a way that pushes her towards whatever path you want her to follow. This is a clunky example, but if you had the bouncer say, “Didn’t you come in with a friend?” Just as Olivia is about to leave, that guilt might send her back to the club. In other words, use those interactions with characters to pivot and guide Olivia so you don’t have to work so hard to have her think her way into and out of the situations. I think seeing how they change the story will be interesting to you. Also, a small note that I got confused about how the girls got to the club and the “I’ll come get you if you need a ride” line — the basics of that need to be clear as they do affect the story. Strong work, Sophia! Thank you!
